current_fact.fact_text: On May 18, 1990, a specially modified French TGV train set a new world rail speed record, hitting 515.3 km/h. For a brief moment, a passenger train blurred into a ground-level comet, pushing the limits of steel, wire, and human engineering.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

current_fact.match_reason: A record-breaking high-speed train fits perfectly with a kinetic, travel-ready episode built around being on the move and embracing momentum.

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 1980, Mount St. Helens erupted catastrophically in Washington State, blowing off its summit, triggering massive landslides, and darkening skies with ash. The event reshaped both the landscape and scientists’ understanding of how quickly a volcano can transform an ecosystem.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: The sudden, explosive power of a volcanic eruption mirrors the raw, high-voltage energy of an ‘electric shock’ rock selection.

queue.fact_text: Also on May 18, 1980, unrest erupted in Miami's predominantly African-American neighborhoods of Overtown and Liberty City after an earlier protest over racial injustice. The unrest laid bare decades of inequality, turning city blocks into a stark, chaotic referendum on civil rights.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: Music that confronts race, identity, and systemic oppression in conscious rap parallels the social tensions and demands for justice underlying the 1980 Miami riots.

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 1993, protests in Copenhagen’s Nørrebro district over the Maastricht Treaty escalated into riots, prompting Danish police to fire live ammunition at civilians for the first time since World War II. Eleven demonstrators were injured, marking a jarring clash between European integration and local dissent.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A politically charged riot over European policy is well matched with an episode dedicated to Rage Against the Machine and music as protest and resistance.

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 1991, northern Somalia broke away and declared itself the Republic of Somaliland. Though still largely unrecognized internationally, it has since built its own institutions, elections, and a distinct sense of nationhood in the Horn of Africa.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: Somaliland’s self-declared independence and resilience in a harsh environment echoes an episode about desert peoples whose music channels survival, identity, and resistance.

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 2018, Cubana de Aviación Flight 972 crashed shortly after takeoff from Havana, killing 112 of the 113 people on board. The tragedy raised deep questions about aviation safety, aging aircraft, and the fragility of lives linked by a single short flight.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A somber aviation disaster pairs with a reflective, cinematic episode about risk, awe, and confronting the thin line between danger and wonder in remote journeys.

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 2006, after the Loktantra Andolan uprising, Nepal’s new government passed a landmark bill stripping the king of most powers and declaring the country secular. It was a dramatic turning point for a Himalayan kingdom long defined by its monarchy and Hindu state identity.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: A nation rethinking monarchy and religion reflects collective learning and unlearning, aligning with an episode about growth, reevaluation, and changing long-held patterns.

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 1974, India conducted its first successful nuclear test under the codename Smiling Buddha, becoming the world’s sixth nuclear-armed state. The serene name masked a profound geopolitical shock that reshaped regional power balances in South Asia.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A deceptively peaceful codename for a powerful nuclear test parallels the contrast between still surfaces and profound underlying shifts in an exploratory, mood-focused mix format.

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 1695, a devastating earthquake struck Linfen in Shanxi during China’s Qing dynasty, killing at least 52,000 people. Entire settlements crumbled, reminding historians how often natural disasters have quietly rewritten local histories.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A catastrophic quake is nature’s violent reminder to slow down and respect the planet’s forces, which contrasts and resonates with an episode inviting a gentler, more attentive pace on a ‘slow planet.’

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 1803, Britain revoked the Treaty of Amiens and declared war on Napoleonic France, ending a short-lived peace in Europe. The decision ushered in more than a decade of near-constant conflict that would redraw borders and topple empires.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: The abrupt restart of a continent-wide war is a historical ‘bomb’ that changed everything, fitting the explosive, origin-story energy of Sedna FM’s very first episode.

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 1794, the Battle of Tourcoing raged in the fields of Flanders during the War of the First Coalition. A confused tangle of armies and shifting alliances ended in a French victory that helped secure the young republic’s northern front.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

queue.match_reason: A smoky, chaotic battlefield in Flanders evokes both fog and flashes of fire, mirroring the episode’s interplay between quiet atmospheres and volcanic intensity.

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 1944, German paratroopers finally evacuated Monte Cassino after four brutal battles in World War II. The ancient abbey, shattered by bombardment and fighting, became a symbol of both cultural loss and hard-fought Allied advance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: After months of destruction and stalemate, Monte Cassino’s conclusion feels like a storm finally breaking, echoing an episode about enduring dark periods and trusting that it can’t rain forever.

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 1926, famed evangelist Aimee Semple McPherson mysteriously disappeared from Venice Beach, California, prompting rumors of kidnapping, escape, and elaborate hoaxes. She reappeared over a month later with a story that only deepened the intrigue.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A celebrity vanishes from public view at the height of media attention; an episode about losing digital connection explores a modern form of disappearance and the stories we project onto it.

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 1804, Napoleon Bonaparte was proclaimed Emperor of the French by the Senate, turning a revolutionary general into a new kind of monarch. His coronation recast France’s experiment with republicanism into an empire that would dominate Europe for years.

episode.title: Morning Drops #30 - Star Treatment
episode.description: A celebration of change and transformation. Gaia traces the evolution of Arctic Monkeys - from raw energy to cinematic sound - reflecting on how artists, and all of us, grow and change through time. A journey through courage, curiosity, and the beauty of slowing down.
songs: Arctic Monkeys - There’d Better Be a Mirrorball

queue.match_reason: Napoleon’s leap from general to emperor is a dramatic transformation in status and image, echoing an episode about artistic evolution and the shifting ‘star treatment’ of public figures.

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 1302, Bruges Matins saw local Flemish militias carry out a nocturnal massacre of the French garrison in Bruges. The bloody surprise attack helped ignite a wider uprising that would shape the region’s struggle for autonomy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A sudden night-time uprising in a medieval city contrasts sharply with the episode’s calming ‘moving meditation’, inviting listeners to reflect on how far our approaches to conflict and change have traveled.

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 1980, the second day of unrest in Miami’s Overtown and Liberty City turned entire neighborhoods into flashpoints, as anger over the acquittal of police officers spilled into the streets. The riots forced the city and country to confront deep racial and economic fractures.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: Streets that became sites of violence and protest contrast with and reframe the idea of public space where music and movement can be empowering and inclusive, as explored in an episode about the dancefloor.

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 1990, while the TGV tore across rails, another kind of speed record was emerging in information: within that same era, the world’s first commercial internet backbone was taking shape, quietly laying tracks for data at light speed. Rail’s physical velocity now had a digital counterpart racing through fiber.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

queue.match_reason: Even as trains and networks chased speed, this episode asks us to slow down and choose how we move through information and sound, turning high-speed eras into intentional experiences.

queue.fact_text: On May 18, 2005, Hubble’s view of Pluto’s moons was possible thanks to a telescope orbiting far above Earth’s own atmospheric turbulence. This same clarity has revealed galaxies colliding, stars being born, and even the spectral fingerprints of alien worlds’ atmospheres.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: Hubble’s deep, cinematic glimpses of the universe pair well with the atmospheric, boundary-blurring textures of trip-hop, both inviting listeners to drift through layered, distant vistas.

queue.fact_text: By May 18, 1991, the newly proclaimed Republic of Somaliland was not just a political declaration but also a quieter cultural experiment. Without broad recognition, its artists, poets, and musicians have helped stitch together a sense of identity in a space that officially does not exist on most maps.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A largely unrecognized African republic quietly shaping its own culture complements an episode about Afrobeat’s rise as a powerful, border-crossing African musical voice.

published.fact_text: In 2005, astronomers using the Hubble Space Telescope confirmed that Pluto has two additional tiny moons, later named Nix and Hydra. Their discovery hinted that even distant dwarf planets can host surprisingly complex miniature systems of satellites.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A discovery of new moons around Pluto mirrors the episode’s theme of reimagining empty space as a canvas for creation and cosmic possibility.

published.fact_text: On May 18, 1969, NASA launched Apollo 10, a full dress rehearsal for the first Moon landing. Its lunar module skimmed just 14.4 km above the surface, giving humanity a near-touch of the Moon before Apollo 11’s historic descent.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: A mission that was literally a rehearsal for a lunar destination aligns with an episode themed around voyaging to Sedna and exploring new territories.

published.fact_text: In 1953, pilot Jacqueline Cochran became the first woman to break the sound barrier, flying an F-86 Sabre at more than 1,050 km/h. Her flight shattered not just air resistance but expectations about what women could do in aviation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: Cochran’s boundary-breaking flight resonates with an episode celebrating women who defied expectations in rock music and carved out loud, powerful spaces.

published.fact_text: On May 18, 1912, Shree Pundalik, directed by Dadasaheb Torne, premiered in Mumbai and is often cited as the first Indian film. Shot on a single reel, it helped ignite a massive cinematic tradition that would grow into one of the world’s largest film industries.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: The birth of Indian cinema pairs with an episode tracing the history of Flamenco; both explore how local art forms evolve into powerful, global cultural expressions.

published.fact_text: In 1499, the Spanish navigator Alonso de Ojeda sailed from Cádiz on a voyage that would bring Europeans to the coasts of what is now Venezuela. His journey helped map South America’s northern shoreline, though it also foreshadowed the colonial upheavals to come.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A coastal voyage from Spain to the Americas connects naturally with an episode framed as a boat trip over shared seas and shorelines.

published.fact_text: On May 18, 1990, a specially modified French TGV train set a new world rail speed record, hitting 515.3 km/h. For a brief moment, a passenger train blurred into a ground-level comet, pushing the limits of steel, wire, and human engineering.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

published.match_reason: A record-breaking high-speed train fits perfectly with a kinetic, travel-ready episode built around being on the move and embracing momentum.
